steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The hypothesis: shoreline hardening harms waterbird communities more than land development does. To support this, we need a comparison where hardening-heavy sites show lower IWCI than development-heavy sites — while keeping the other variable low so we can isolate each factor.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Back River (high development, low hardening) scores higher IWCI than Margothy (low development, high hardening)': less hardening yields better community integrity; more hardening yields worse. This directly supports the hypothesis. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Langford (high development, low hardening) scores lower IWCI than Mill (low development, high hardening)': the opposite pattern — hardening sites score higher, which contradicts the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Stony/Curtis (both high) vs. Ware/Honga (both low)': both variables move together; it is impossible to isolate hardening's effect. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Old Road (both high) scores lower than Onancock (both low)': again, both variables differ at once — could be development, hardening, or both. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
